spottedIssue: Spotted something wrong on this page? Email me.

landing.seoDescription: The design system behind warrendeleon.com: colour, typography, layout, components and accessibility, documented from the live stylesheet.
landing.title: Design system
landing.intro: The system this site is built from, documented from the live stylesheet rather than written next to it. Foundations set the raw material. The rest shows how it comes together.

principles.title: Quiet
principles.body: One accent, used with intent. Green means interaction or emphasis and nothing else, so anything green on the page either invites a tap or marks what matters. Everything around it is black, white and grey.

principles.title: Editorial
principles.body: Content leads. Space comes before chrome and type before decoration. The layout stays out of the way so the writing and the work can be read.

principles.title: Technical
principles.body: Every value is a CSS variable, and these pages read from that same source when the site builds. The documentation cannot drift from the site because it is generated from it.

cardBlurbs.colour: One brand green, a neutral scale, and the token that keeps green readable.
cardBlurbs.typography: One typeface, a role-based scale that steps down per screen.
cardBlurbs.layout: Three columns, honest breakpoints, a four-pixel spacing ramp.
cardBlurbs.shape-elevation: The radius scale, the themed shadows, the hairline rule.
cardBlurbs.iconography: Feather-style line icons and solid brand marks.
cardBlurbs.motion: Short, quiet transitions, and the reduced-motion switch.

componentBlurbs.buttons: Two tiers, real states, and what to write on them.
componentBlurbs.tags-chips: Badges that state a fact, tags that go somewhere.
componentBlurbs.cards: From the base card to the blog's lead and series cards.
componentBlurbs.navigation: One chrome set reshaping across three viewports.
componentBlurbs.sheets-overlays: The bottom-sheet primitive behind every picker.
componentBlurbs.article-patterns: Callouts, code, tables, contents — the long-form kit.
componentBlurbs.timeline: The work-experience rail, logo dots and all.
componentBlurbs.media: The photo carousel, the device frame, and placeholders.

landing.moreBody: Content guidelines and accessibility standards are being written up next, in the same shape as the pages here.
landing.livingBody: The colour, type and spacing tables on these pages are generated from the site's stylesheet at build time. Change a token in the CSS and the tables here change with it. If the parser can no longer read the stylesheet, the build fails rather than showing an empty table.

colour.seoTitle: Colour | Design system | Warren de Leon
colour.seoDescription: The colour system behind warrendeleon.com: one brand green, a theme-aware neutral scale, and the readable-green token, documented from the live CSS.
colour.title: Colour
colour.intro: One brand green does all the accent work. Everything else is a neutral scale that flips between light and dark. The values below are read from the stylesheet at build time.

usage.--color-green: The one accent: primary buttons, active tab, the title bar, focus rings.
usage.--color-green-dark: Hover and pressed green on solid buttons.
usage.--color-green-text: Green for text and links: readable on light, brand lime on dark.
usage.--color-green-text-hover: Hover green for text and links.
usage.--color-white: Pure white, mostly for text on solid green.
usage.--bg-secondary: Tinted bands, specimen stages, code headers.
usage.--device-edge: The outer ring on the phone frame.

colour.brandBody: The green is the single accent. It fills primary buttons, marks the active tab, draws the bar under a page title and rings a focused control. Because it means only those things, a reader learns to read green as “this does something”.
colour.brandTextNote: Lime green is bright, and at body-text size on a white background it does not meet the contrast bar. That is why a separate text green exists: light mode steps the green down for text and links, while dark mode keeps the brand lime. The reason it exists is set out on the accessibility page.
colour.neutralsBody: The neutral tokens carry two values, one per theme, so a surface is designed in both from the start. Text sits at three weights of emphasis; backgrounds step from the page, to tinted bands, to raised cards.
colour.interactionBody: Links and solid buttons move through the greens on hover. The states are the same everywhere, so a control always tells you what it will do.
colour.interactionDisabledNote: Reduced opacity; the colour does not change.
colour.calloutsBody: Long-form articles use four callout colours to flag a trap, an aside, a caution or a neutral note. These are the one place a colour other than green carries meaning.
colour.calloutsScoping: Scope: callout colours live in article prose only. They never appear in the interface chrome, where green and the neutrals do all the work.
colour.calloutsException: These docs make one deliberate exception to that rule. The Do and Don’t cards further down use green and red, because a design system documenting itself is the one place those signals belong outside prose.

calloutsUses.danger: A mistake or trap worth stopping the reader on.
calloutsUses.tip: A helpful aside that is not essential.
calloutsUses.info: Neutral context or a pointer elsewhere.

dos: Let green mean one thing: interaction or emphasis. If it is green, it should be tappable or worth noticing.
Design every surface in both themes at once. The token pairs make that the default, not an afterthought.
Use the text green for green text and links, so it stays readable on a light background.
donts: Introduce a second accent. A new colour steals the meaning green is carrying.
Paint the brand lime onto small text on white. It fails the contrast bar; that is what the text green is for.
Reach for a callout colour in the interface. Those belong in article prose.
colour.elevationNote: Elevation tokens (the themed shadows) are documented on the Shape & elevation page.

typography.seoTitle: Typography | Design system | Warren de Leon
typography.seoDescription: The type system behind warrendeleon.com: one typeface, a role-based scale that steps down per screen, documented from the live CSS.
typography.title: Typography
typography.intro: One typeface, a handful of weights, and a role-based scale that steps down a notch on smaller screens. Every size below is a CSS variable read from the stylesheet.
typography.familyBody: The interface is set in Inter, self-hosted rather than pulled from a font service so the first paint never waits on a third-party request. There is no display or heading font: weight and size carry the hierarchy. Headings run at 800, sub-headings and labels at 700, buttons and emphasis at 600, and body copy at 400.
typography.familyStackNote: The full stack falls back to the system sans-serif if Inter has not loaded, so text is always readable.
typography.scaleBody: Sizes are roles, not one-offs. The same variable drives every page title, every section heading, and so on. The three columns are the desktop value and the two step-downs, applied at the tablet and mobile breakpoints on the Layout page.

usage.--fs-h1: Page title, with the green bar.
usage.--bar-h1: Width of the green bar under the title.

typography.specimensBody: Rendered at their live sizes. Narrow the window and they step down exactly as the site does.

samples.body: Body copy sets the reading pace outside articles: long enough to breathe, short enough to scan. This is the size most words on the site are read at.
samples.prose: Article reading size, a touch larger and looser, tuned for a long sit with a single column of text.

specimenNotes.h1: Page title. Carries the green bar beneath it.
specimenNotes.proseH2: Article section heading, with the green underline.

typography.monoBody: A monospace stack marks anything machine-set: meta lines and dates, tags and badges, table headers, code, and the small uppercase kickers. It signals “this is data, not prose”. The stack is SF Mono, Fira Code, Cascadia Code, then the system monospace.

mapping.where: The h1 on every top-level page, with the green bar.
Band headings, with the short green underline.
The h2 inside a blog post, with the green underline. Article h2 only; sub-headings drop to a plain weight.
About text, page intros, card copy.
The reading column of an article.

typography.headingsBody: Two green marks, two jobs. Page and section titles carry a short green bar under the first words — a fixed-width accent that does not stretch with the text. Article headings instead take a full underline that runs the width of the words. If the mark spans the text, you are inside an article; if it is a short bar, you are looking at a page or section title.

headingsLabels.pageTitle: Page title: the bar is --bar-h1 wide, never the text width. Class .section-title.
headingsLabels.sectionHeading: Section heading: a fixed 60px bar. Class .section-heading.
headingsLabels.articleHeading: Article heading: a full underline across the text. .prose h2.
headingsLabels.browline: Detail browline: green text, no mark. .prose h3.

layout.seoTitle: Layout | Design system | Warren de Leon
layout.seoDescription: The layout system behind warrendeleon.com: three content columns, honest breakpoints, and a four-pixel spacing ramp.
layout.title: Layout
layout.intro: Mobile first, one column, content centred while the gutter flexes. Three fixed columns cover phone, tablet and desktop. The spacing ramp below is read from the stylesheet.
layout.columnsBody: The page is one centred column at a fixed maximum width per band. Below that width the gutter is the only thing that flexes, so the reading measure stays comfortable rather than stretching edge to edge on a wide screen. Articles narrow further, to a 720-pixel reading column; only full-bleed blocks like code break out of it.
layout.breakpointsBody: Three bands, stated as they really are. The tablet band runs up to 1279 pixels, so a 1024-pixel window gets tablet sizing, not desktop. The tab dock is a phone and tablet affordance; the desktop nav takes over at 1280.
layout.spacingBody: Space is built on a four-pixel base. The tokens below set the rhythm between sections and headings; because section padding is one-sided, two adjacent sections sit twice the token apart.

spacingUsage.--sp-section: Padding on a section (two stack at twice this).
spacingUsage.--sp-heading: Gap from a section heading to its content.

layout.nestingBody: The rhythm nests: section, then card, then group, then element, each with less air than the one around it.
layout.targetsBody: Primary touch controls hold a 44-pixel floor: the dock tabs, the reading bar's controls, the footer icons on a phone. The glyph inside stays 15 to 20 pixels; padding does the rest. Compact, pointer-first controls — the shelf and series arrows, the top bar's search — sit at 38 to 42 pixels with clear space around them.
layout.logoBody: The mark sits in a circular frame, filling roughly two-thirds of it with a hairline ring around the edge. It appears at 48 pixels beside a role, 96 in a card, and 120 as a timeline anchor.
layout.annotatedBody: The hatched bands are the measured space itself, not drawings of it: their heights are driven by the live tokens, so they step down per band exactly as the site does. Labels read desktop / tablet / mobile.

annotatedLabels.sections: Two adjacent sections: each brings its own one-sided padding, so the content sits two tokens apart.
annotatedLabels.heading: A section heading to its content: one --sp-heading.
annotatedLabels.card: The base card’s padding: a fixed 24px on every side.
annotatedLabels.buttons: Stacked pill CTAs: a fixed 10px gap.

layout.perBandCaption: Each token at its mobile, tablet and desktop size — the parsed values side by side, whatever device you are on. The figures below then resize live with your viewport.
layout.targetsCaption: Three real sizes from the chrome, glyphs at 18px: padding carries the target, not bigger drawings.
layout.logoCaption: The mark fills roughly two-thirds of a white disc with a hairline green ring — 48px beside a role or on the phone timeline, 96 in a card, 120 as the desktop timeline anchor. Live in .timeline-logo and .current-role-logo.

shapeElevation.seoTitle: Shape & elevation | Design system | Warren de Leon
shapeElevation.seoDescription: The shape and elevation language behind warrendeleon.com: the radius scale, the theme-aware shadows, and the hairline-border rule.
shapeElevation.title: Shape & elevation
shapeElevation.intro: Corners round on a consistent scale, shadows are quiet and theme-aware, and most separation is done with a single hairline rather than a shadow.
shapeElevation.radiusBody: Radius climbs with the size of the surface: the smallest on an inline code chip, the largest on a bottom sheet. These are recurring literal values in the component styles rather than tokens, so they are documented here by hand and checked against the stylesheet.

radiusRows.where: Lead post card, mobile role card

shapeElevation.radiusNote: The tag chip and the badge sit a pixel apart, at 7 and 8 pixels. It is a small inconsistency worth either keeping on purpose or closing; for now the docs report both as they ship.
shapeElevation.shadowsBody: Shadows are soft and low. The signature rule is that dark mode does not darken: a black shadow on a dark page reads as nothing, so the dark tokens carry a faint light shadow instead. Each token below is shown on both a light and a dark stage.

shadowsUsage.--shadow: Legacy card lift, still on cards and feed posts.

shapeElevation.bordersBody: The default separator is a one-pixel hairline in the border colour, not a shadow. Cards, tables, sheets and bands are all framed this way. Shadows are reserved for things that genuinely float above the page, like the dock or an open sheet.

iconography.seoTitle: Iconography | Design system | Warren de Leon
iconography.seoDescription: The icon language behind warrendeleon.com: Feather-style line icons and solid brand marks, all in currentColor.
iconography.title: Iconography
iconography.intro: Two kinds of icon. Interface actions use light line icons in the Feather style; brand and social links use solid marks. Both take their colour from the text around them.
iconography.lineBody: Interface icons are drawn on a 24-unit grid with a two-pixel stroke, round caps and round joins, in the Feather style. They render at 15 to 20 pixels inside a hit area of at least 38, and never carry their own colour: they inherit currentColor from the text.
iconography.lineCaption: A sample of the interface set, at the size they ship.
iconography.brandBody: Social and brand icons are solid single-colour marks rather than line drawings, so a recognisable logo stays recognisable. They live in the footer and inherit the same currentColor, greening on hover. This is the real component, rendered here from the same source the footer uses.
dos: Let icons take currentColor, so they track the theme and the text around them.
Keep a 44-pixel hit area even when the glyph is 16 pixels.
Use line icons for actions and solid marks for brands.
donts: Hard-code an icon's colour. It should follow the text.
Mix stroke and fill styles within the interface set.
Shrink the tappable area to the size of the drawing.
iconography.hitCaption: The hit area, annotated: a 20px glyph centred in a 44px square — the hatched ring is the tappable padding.

motion.seoTitle: Motion | Design system | Warren de Leon
motion.seoDescription: The motion language behind warrendeleon.com: short, quiet transitions and a firm reduced-motion switch.
motion.title: Motion
motion.intro: Motion is quiet and quick. It confirms an action or eases a change of state; it is never decoration. And it stops the moment a visitor asks for less.
motion.durationsBody: Most transitions run at a fifth of a second on an ease curve: a colour shift on hover, a border settling in. The theme swap runs a little longer so a whole-page repaint does not snap. Pages fade up a few pixels on load. Bottom sheets are the one expressive move, sliding in on a spring curve.
motion.reducedBody: When a visitor's system asks for reduced motion, the site honours it without exception. Animations and transitions collapse to a hair over zero, and smooth scrolling reverts to a jump. This is the rule, not a nicety:

frameThemeNote: The frames below are the real pages rendered at real widths, scripts off — so they always show the light theme and no analytics run inside them.

buttons.seoTitle: Buttons | Design system | Warren de Leon
buttons.seoDescription: The two button tiers on warrendeleon.com: inline buttons for forms and actions, and pill CTAs for page-level calls to action, with states and content rules.
buttons.title: Buttons
buttons.intro: Two tiers. Inline buttons carry ordinary actions inside forms and cards; pill CTAs carry the one or two calls to action that anchor a whole page. Knowing which tier a control belongs to is most of the decision.
buttons.inlineBody: The default control: an 8-pixel-radius button for actions inside forms, cards and prose. Solid green for the primary action, outlined green for the secondary. One primary per group.
buttons.pillBody: Taller 48-pixel pills for the page-level calls to action — the ones a visitor should see from across the room, like “Download CV” or “Book a call”. Solid and outline, same green pair.
buttons.statesBody: Every state, rendered. Hover deepens the green; focus draws the shared 2-pixel green ring; a disabled control drops to a low opacity without changing hue, so it still reads as the same control. The hover and focus columns show those states applied, since a static page cannot hold a cursor over itself.
buttons.statesDisabledNote: Most buttons on the site are links, so a true disabled state is rare; the series arrows are the live example of this convention.
buttons.contentBody: Labels are a verb and a noun, in sentence case, on one line. Say what happens, from the reader's side, without “my” or “your”.
dos: Use one primary button per group; make the rest outline or plain.
Reserve pill CTAs for page-level actions; use inline buttons everywhere else.
Label with a verb and a noun: “Download CV”, “Book a call”.
donts: Stack two solid-green buttons side by side — nothing leads.
Write “Click here” or “Submit”; say what the button does.
Grey out a disabled button so hard it stops looking like the control.
buttons.whereBody: Inline: .btn with .btn-primary or .btn-outline. Pills: .hero-cta with .hero-cta-solid or .hero-cta-outline, grouped in .hero-ctas.

tagsChips.seoTitle: Tags & chips | Design system | Warren de Leon
tagsChips.seoDescription: The small pill family on warrendeleon.com: badges for static metadata and post tags for taxonomy links, and when to reach for each.
tagsChips.title: Tags & chips
tagsChips.intro: Small rounded pills that label things. They look alike on purpose, but they do different jobs: a badge states a fact, a tag links to more.
tagsChips.badgeBody: Static metadata that is not a link: a technology on a work-experience card, a language on a post. Neutral fill, 8-pixel radius, no hover.
tagsChips.tagBody: A taxonomy link, one pixel tighter at the corners than a badge. It greens its border and text on hover because it takes you somewhere; a badge does not.
dos: Use a badge for a fact that is not clickable.
Use a post tag when the pill navigates to a filtered list.
Group related pills with a small, even gap.
donts: Style a badge to look tappable when nothing happens on tap.
Fill a pill with the brand green; the neutral fill is deliberate.
Crowd a row with so many pills that none can be read.
tagsChips.whereBody: Badge: .badge (grouped in .badge-group). Post tag: .post-tag (grouped in .post-tags). Both share the neutral --badge-bg / --badge-text pair.

cards.seoTitle: Cards | Design system | Warren de Leon
cards.seoDescription: The card family on warrendeleon.com: the base card and the blog feed post, with the whole-surface link and hover-lift rules.
cards.title: Cards
cards.intro: A card groups a small unit of content behind a hairline border and a soft shadow. The blog feed post is the card doing its most-used job.
cards.baseBody: A rounded 12-pixel surface on the card background, framed by a hairline and lifted by a soft shadow. On hover it lifts a little more. It is the shell almost every other card is built on.
cards.feedBody: The blog listing card — the real FeedPostCard component, here with a placeholder where the hero image goes. On desktop it is a thumbnail beside a title, meta line and two-line summary; on phones it collapses to a compact row.
cards.wholeLinkNote: The entire card is a single link, not a title link with a separate “read more”. Hover lifts the card and greens the title together.
cards.seriesHeroBody: The blog home's current-series card, rendered live: hero art beside the series pitch, with the published-of-total progress bar. On phones it stacks and gains a solid View series button.
cards.leadBody: The newest post gets a larger lead treatment, and the whole feed reshapes per band — a big lead card over compact rows on a phone, a two-column grid on a tablet, thumbnail rows with a sidebar on desktop. The frames below are the real blog page at each width.
dos: Make the whole card one link when it points at one place.
Give only the newest post the larger lead card.
Let the hover lift and the green title move together.
donts: Put several competing links inside one card surface.
Give every post the lead treatment; nothing stands out then.
Drop the border and rely on shadow alone to separate cards.
cards.seriesCardBody: The More-series shelf card from Blog home: art over a parts count, title and a two-line description. The real SeriesCard component, here with a placeholder for the art.
cards.relatedBody: The More-from-the-blog shelf card that closes a post: image, date, title, and the whole surface one link. The real RelatedCard component.
cards.authorBody: The bio card at the end of every post — avatar, name, bio, profile link. The real AuthorCard component, rendered live.
cards.roleBody: The home page's latest-role card: a logo disc beside the company and role, description below. Rendered from the real classes with placeholder content.

navigation.seoTitle: Navigation | Design system | Warren de Leon
navigation.seoDescription: How you move around warrendeleon.com: the top bar, the desktop nav, the one-element tab dock, the contextual action bar, and the footer.
navigation.title: Navigation
navigation.intro: Getting around the site uses one chrome set that reshapes per screen rather than several separate ones. The same dock is edge-to-edge on a phone, a floating pill on a tablet, and gone on desktop, where the top nav takes over.
navigation.viewportsBody: Navigation is shaped by the viewport more than any other part of the system. The frames below are the real home page rendered at each band's width, whatever device you are reading this on: the dock runs edge-to-edge on a phone, floats as a pill on a tablet, and gives way to the top nav links on desktop.
navigation.barBody: A sticky bar carrying the logo and the utility controls (language, theme, and blog search). It stands 64 pixels tall on desktop, 60 on tablet and 56 on a phone. On desktop it also holds the three section links; below 1280 those move to the dock.
navigation.dockBody: The primary section navigation on phone and tablet: four tabs — Home, Blog, Work, Book a call. It is a single element with one breakpoint, not two components. The frames below are the real page cropped to the dock: edge-to-edge at 390 with the active tab in green text alone, a floating pill at 768 where the active tab gains a tinted fill. Four tabs is structural; a fifth would not fit the grid, which is why this design-system section lives in the footer.
navigation.actionBody: On a reading page the dock is replaced, not joined, by a contextual action bar: a progress ring with the current section opens the contents sheet, then share and back-to-top. This is the real ReadingBar component, shown mid-article. One bar at a time: browse chrome or read chrome, never both.
navigation.footerBody: A single centred column on every screen: the section links, one flat row of social marks with RSS, and the copyright. No logo, ever — the top bar already carries it. The live footer is at the bottom of this very page.
navigation.railBody: This section's own navigation is part of the system too: a grouped rail on desktop (Foundations, Components and so on as labelled blocks), a collapsible section menu above the page title below 1280 pixels, plus the breadcrumb and the On-this-page card.
dos: Keep the dock to four tabs; put anything else in the footer.
Swap the dock for the action bar on reading pages, never show both.
Let the top bar hold utilities; let the dock or nav hold sections.
donts: Add a fifth tab to the dock.
Stack the dock and the action bar on the same screen.
Put a logo in the footer; the top bar owns it.
navigation.whereBody: Nav.astro (.nav), TabBar.astro, ReadingBar.astro and Footer.astro (.footer). The dock and reading bar above are those components rendered live; the frames show the top bar in place.

sheetsOverlays.seoTitle: Sheets & overlays | Design system | Warren de Leon
sheetsOverlays.seoDescription: The bottom-sheet pattern on warrendeleon.com: a shared primitive for pickers and menus on touch, with a desktop dropdown equivalent.
sheetsOverlays.title: Sheets & overlays
sheetsOverlays.intro: On touch, choices that would be a dropdown on desktop slide up from the bottom as a sheet. One primitive backs the contents menu, the share menu and the language picker.
sheetsOverlays.anatomyBody: A panel rounded 20 pixels at the top, capped by a 36-by-4 grabber, over a dimmed backdrop. Rows are 48 pixels tall for a comfortable thumb; the active row carries a faint green tint. It slides in on a spring curve, so it arrives with a little weight.
sheetsOverlays.anatomyCaption: The sheet's rows and grabber, from the real classes.
sheetsOverlays.desktopBody: The same picker is a dropdown on desktop, not a sheet. The choice is the same; only the surface changes to suit a pointer and a bigger screen. Full-screen media zoom is the one overlay that stays the same on every device.
dos: Reach for a sheet when a touch user picks from a short list.
Keep rows at 48 pixels so a thumb lands cleanly.
Render the same choice as a dropdown on desktop.
donts: Use a sheet for a single yes/no; that is a dialog.
Shrink the rows below a comfortable touch size.
Trap the reader: a tap on the backdrop closes the sheet.
sheetsOverlays.whereBody: The .post-sheet family in global.css: .post-sheet-panel, .post-sheet-grabber, .post-sheet-head, .post-sheet-list, with .sheet-active on the current row.

articlePatterns.seoTitle: Article patterns | Design system | Warren de Leon
articlePatterns.seoDescription: The long-form kit behind the blog on warrendeleon.com: callouts, code blocks, the table doctrine, and the subscribe form.
articlePatterns.title: Article patterns
articlePatterns.intro: The kit a blog post is built from. These are the patterns that turn a wall of text into something you can read, scan and trust.
articlePatterns.calloutsBody: Four flags for a trap, a tip, a caution or a neutral note, each with its own colour and icon. They pull a single important point out of the flow without breaking it.

calloutSamples.danger: A red flag stops you on a mistake worth avoiding.
calloutSamples.tip: A tip offers a helpful aside you can take or leave.
calloutSamples.warning: A warning marks something to do with care.
calloutSamples.info: Info gives neutral context or points elsewhere.

articlePatterns.quoteBody: A pulled quote, set off by a green left rule on a tinted panel. Used for a real quotation, not for emphasis — emphasis is what bold is for.
articlePatterns.quoteSample: The best engineering leaders never fully leave the craft.
articlePatterns.codeBody: Inline code sits in a tinted chip. Blocks get a header with the language and Copy and Expand controls, syntax-highlighted with Shiki in a light and a dark theme, and they run full-bleed to the screen edges on a phone so a wide line has room.
articlePatterns.tablesBody: An article never uses a bare HTML table. A classic table ships inside a bordered card with a sticky first column and a monospace header strip; and where rows would fight the phone column, the data becomes a bespoke card instead. All five patterns, rendered from the real classes:

rows.desc: Meets the bar with gaps a reviewer would flag.
Meets the bar cleanly; nothing to flag.

items.text: Every value from the live stylesheet
A lookalike rebuilt with new CSS

rows.name: Focus visible
rows.desc: Every interactive control draws the shared green ring.

rows.name: Target size
rows.desc: Hit areas stay at 44 pixels or better.

articlePatterns.contentsBody: A long article carries its table of contents: the real Toc component renders as a sticky sidebar card from 1024 pixels, and the same headings feed the contents sheet behind the reading bar's progress ring below that.
articlePatterns.seriesNavBody: A post that belongs to a series opens with a horizontal shelf of numbered part cards — the current part outlined green, the rest linking across the series. Mobile scrolls the shelf freely; desktop adds arrow controls.
articlePatterns.subscribeBody: The newsletter band that closes a post: a short pitch, one email field, and a note about double opt-in. This is the real component, rendered live.
articlePatterns.whereBody: global.css: .prose blockquote.callout-*, .code-block-* and .prose pre, the .dt-card family, plus SubscribeForm.astro.
articlePatterns.diagramsBody: Architecture diagrams are written as Mermaid in the article source and pre-rendered to SVG at authoring time — no diagram library ships to the reader. The site's own tokens theme the result (green nodes, neutral arrows), so it re-colours with the theme like everything else, and the corner control opens it full screen. This is a real diagram from a published article:

media.seoTitle: Media | Design system | Warren de Leon
media.seoDescription: The media patterns on warrendeleon.com: the photo carousel, the flat device frame for app screenshots, full-screen zoom, and image placeholders.
media.title: Media
media.intro: How photos and screenshots are carried: a carousel on the home hero, a flat green-railed phone frame for app screenshots, a full-screen zoom for diagrams and images, and a placeholder for specimens.
media.carouselBody: The home hero's photo carousel, rendered live: arrows and a counter on the frame, a dot row beneath it from 700 pixels (the active dot stretches to a pill), swipe on touch, and autoplay that respects reduced motion and pauses from the control beside the dots. On a phone the dots give way to an overlay title on the photo.
media.deviceBody: App screenshots in articles sit inside a flat phone frame drawn in CSS: a brand-green rail, a thin black bezel, side buttons drawn just outside the rail, and a theme-aware shadow. It holds to the blog's flat aesthetic rather than a photoreal render.
media.deviceCaption: The .device-frame pattern, here with a placeholder where the screenshot goes.
media.zoomBody: Images and pre-rendered diagrams in articles open in a full-screen lightbox on tap — the one overlay that behaves the same on every device. Diagrams re-theme inside it, so a dark-mode reader zooms into a dark-mode diagram.
media.placeholderBody: Specimens on these docs that would carry a photo use a neutral placeholder rather than a borrowed image, so the layout is honest about what is content and what is chrome.
media.placeholderCaption: The placeholder at card-thumbnail and hero ratios.

timeline.seoTitle: Timeline | Design system | Warren de Leon
timeline.seoDescription: The work-experience timeline on warrendeleon.com: a vertical rail of logo dots and cards, with a Latest tag and a per-screen dot scale.
timeline.title: Timeline
timeline.intro: The work-experience rail: a vertical line with a company logo riding it at each stop, and a card beside each logo. Below is the real component fed placeholder entries — on the live page it carries the real employment record.
timeline.anatomyBody: A logo circle sits on the rail at each company, ringed in green — 120 pixels on desktop, 92 on tablet, 48 on a phone. A green “Latest” tag caps the top. Each card carries the company in green, a monospace date range, the role, a short summary and a row of tech chips; a chevron marks it as a link. On a phone the card compacts to logo, company, role and date.
timeline.detailNote: On a company's own page the logo dot anchors the page title directly — the one place a page heading drops its green bar, because the dot already marks it.
dos: Use the timeline for a sequence of dated entries on one rail.
Keep the newest at the top, under the Latest tag.
Let each card be one link to the full entry.
donts: Reach for the timeline for unordered items; that is a list.
Show the positions badge on a company with a single role.
Keep the green bar on a detail page where the logo dot already anchors the title.
timeline.whereBody: Timeline.astro and TimelineEntry.astro (.timeline, .timeline-card, .timeline-logo), fed from the work-experience data.

sample.title: Engineering Manager
sample.desc: A placeholder role showing the card anatomy: a one-line summary that truncates after a few words so the row stays scannable.

sample.title: Senior Engineer
sample.desc: An earlier placeholder role, with a closed date range and the same compact card.
